{{Short description|Judgments about a person based on their accent}}
[[Accent (sociolinguistics)|Accent]]s are the distinctive variations in the pronunciation of a language. They can be native or foreign, local or national and can provide information about a person’s geographical  [[locality (settlement)|locality]], [[socio-economic status]] and [[ethnicity]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Labov|first=W.|title=The social stratification of English in New York City|year=2006|publisher=Cambridge University Press|location=Cambridge [UK]|isbn=978-0-521-82122-3|edition=2nd}}</ref> The '''perception of accents''' is normal within any given group of language users and involves the categorisation of speakers into [[social groups]] and entails judgments about the accented speaker, including their status<ref>{{cite journal|last=Ross|first=A.|title=Linguistic class-indicators in present-day English|journal=Neuphilologische Mitteilungen|year=1954|volume=16|pages=171–185}}</ref> and [[personality]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dailey|first=R. M.|author2=Giles, Howard|author3= Jansma, Laura L.|title=Language attitudes in an Anglo-Hispanic context: the role of the linguistic landscape|journal=Language & Communication|year=2005|volume=25|issue=1|pages=27–38|doi=10.1016/j.langcom.2004.04.004}}</ref> Thus, accent perception is deeply tied to [[language ideologies]]. Accents can significantly alter the perception of an individual or an entire group, which is an important fact considering that the frequency that people with different accents are encountering one another is increasing, partially due to inexpensive international travel and [[social media]]. As well as affecting judgments, accents also affect key  [[Cognition|cognitive processes]] (e.g., [[memory]]) that are involved in a myriad of daily activities. The development of accent perception occurs in early childhood. Consequently, from a young age accents influence our perception of other people, decisions we make about when and how to interact with others, and, in reciprocal fashion, how other people perceive us.

== Social identity theory of accents ==

[[Social identity theory]] is a theory that describes intergroup behaviour based on group membership. Markers of group membership can be arbitrary, e.g., coloured vests, a flip of a coin, etc., or non-arbitrary, e.g., gender, language, race, etc.<ref>{{cite book|last=Lindzey|first=D. T.|author2=Gilbert, S. T.|author3= Fiske, G.|title=The handbook of social psychology|year=1998|publisher=McGraw-Hill|location=Boston, Massachusetts |isbn=978-0-19-521376-8|pages=357–411|edition=4th}}</ref> Accent is a non-arbitrary marker for group membership that is potentially more salient than most other non-arbitrary markers such as race<ref>{{cite journal|last=Kurzban|first=R.|author2=Tooby, J.|author3= Cosmides, L.|title=Can race be erased? Coalitional computation and social categorization|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|year=2001|volume=98|issue=26|pages=15387–15392|doi=10.1073/pnas.251541498|pmid=11742078|pmc=65039|bibcode=2001PNAS...9815387K|doi-access=free}}</ref> and visual cues in general.<ref>{{Cite journal
| last = Rakić
| first = T.
|author2= Steffens, M. C.|author3= Mummendey, A.
| title = Blinded by the accent! The minor role of looks in ethnic categorization
| journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology

| year = 2011
| volume = 100
| issue = 1
| pages = 16–29
| doi = 10.1037/a0021522
| pmid=21038973
}}</ref> One component of social identity theory states that members of the same group will treat and judge other members of their group ([[in-group]] members) preferentially compared to those who are not in their group ([[Ingroups and outgroups|out-group]] members).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Tajfel|first=H.|author2=Billig, M. G.|author3= Bundy, R. P.|author4= Flament, C.|title=Social categorization and intergroup behaviour|journal=European Journal of Social Psychology|year=1971|volume=1|issue=2|pages=149–178|doi=10.1002/ejsp.2420010202}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Billig|first=M.|author2=Tajfel, H.|title=Social categorization and similarity in intergroup behaviour|journal=European Journal of Social Psychology|year=1973|volume=3|issue=1|pages=27–52|doi=10.1002/ejsp.2420030103}}</ref> This phenomenon is called [[in-group bias]] and when applied to accents is called the own-accent bias. There are many examples of the discrimination of out-groups based on language, e.g., the banning of the  [[German language in the United States#Persecution during World War I|public speaking of German]] in the [[United States]] during [[World War I]]  and the [[Al-Anfal Campaign]], however, there are also examples of discrimination based on accent. Some of these instances date back many several millennia, for example, in the [[Bible]] in Judges 12:5-6 the following quote depicting the mass-killing of a people based on their accent appears:

:“The Gileadites captured the fords of the Jordan leading to Ephraim, and whenever a survivor of Ephraim said, “Let me cross over,” the men of Gilead asked him, “Are you an Ephraimite?” If he replied, “No,” they said, “All right, say ‘[[Shibboleth]].’” If he said, “Sibboleth,” because he could not pronounce the word correctly, they seized him and killed him at the fords of the Jordan. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites were killed at that time.”

Whereas some are more recent, for example, in his play [[Pygmalion (play)|Pygmalion]] [[George Bernard Shaw]] famously recognised the disparities of accent (even in a native context) when he wrote:

:“It is impossible for an Englishman to open his mouth without making some other Englishman hate or despise him”.<ref>{{cite book|last=Shaw|first=B.|title=Pygmalion : a romance in five acts : definitive text|year=2008|publisher=Methuen Drama|location=London|isbn=9780713679977}}</ref>

== Evolutionary underpinnings of the own-accent bias ==

Accents function as markers of social group membership broadcasting information about individuals' in-group/out-group status. However, unlike other seemingly more conspicuous non-arbitrary markers (e.g., race), the accent an individual has is not outwardly obvious to a casual observer unless the individual speaks and is within hearing range of the observer. This raises the question of how such an easily hidden characteristic became a marker of group membership in the first place. One predominant account suggests an answer to this conundrum lies in  [[Evolutionary history of life|evolutionary history]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Baker|first=M. C.|title=The atoms of language.|year=2001|publisher=Basic Books|location=New York, USA|isbn=978-0465005222}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Henrich|first=N.|title=Why humans cooperate a cultural and evolutionary explanation|year=2007|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Oxford|isbn=978-0195314236|author2=Henrich, J.}}</ref> In modern societies people of many different racial backgrounds live together, which provides modern humans with the chance to experience a wide range of races and racial characteristics (e.g., different coloured skin).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Messick|first=D. M.|author2=Mackie, D. M.|title=Intergroup relations|journal=Annual Review of Psychology|year=1989|volume=40|issue=1|pages=45–81|doi=10.1146/annurev.ps.40.020189.000401|pmid=2648982}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Stangor|first=C.|author2=Lynch, L.|author3= Duan, C.|author4= Glas, B.|title=Categorization of individuals on the basis of multiple social features|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=1992|volume=62|issue=2|pages=207–218|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.62.2.207}}</ref> However, in early societies neighbouring communities could not travel far except by walking, thus they were likely to look similar.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Cosmides|first=L.|author2=Tooby, J.|author3= Kurzban, R.|title=Perceptions of race|journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences|year=2003|volume=7|issue=4|pages=173–179|doi=10.1016/S1364-6613(03)00057-3|pmid=12691766|s2cid=11343153}}</ref> As such, a [[natural selection]] pressure may have existed that favoured social attention to accents, which functioned as an  [[Signalling theory#Honest signals|honesty signal]] (i.e., an honest signal of an individual's group membership), so individuals could easily identify in-group members from the potential threat of out-group members.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Cohen|first=E.|title=The evolution of tag-based cooperation in humans|journal=Current Anthropology|year=2012|volume=53|issue=5|pages=588–616|doi=10.1086/667654|hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0010-1FDD-D|hdl-access=free}}</ref>  In comparison, the  [[Evolutionary pressure|selection pressure]] to socially attend to race was less relevant.<ref name="Accent Trumps Race in Guiding Children's Social Preferences">{{cite journal|last=Kinzler|first=K. D.|author2=Shutts, K.|author3= DeJesus, J.|author4= Spelke, E. S.|title=Accent trumps race in guiding children's social preferences|journal=Social Cognition|year=2009|volume=27|issue=4|pages=623–634|doi=10.1521/soco.2009.27.4.623|pmid=21603154|pmc=3096936}}</ref>

== Theories of own-accent bias ==

The ''own-accent bias'' is the inclination toward, and more positive judgement of, individuals with the same accent as yourself compared to those with a different accent. There are two main theories that attempt to explain this bias: affective processing and prototype representation.

=== Affective processing ===

The affective processing approach proposes that the positive-bias exhibited for others who speak with an own-accent is produced by a (potentially  [[Unconscious mind|unconscious]])  [[emotion]]al reaction. Put simply, people like others who have the same accent as themselves for that precise reason; they like it. This theory has developed, and draws support, from  [[neuroscience|neuroscientific]] research investigating affective  [[prosody (linguistics)|prosody]] (a key component underlying accent) and vocal emotion, which has found activation (predominantly in the right  [[Cerebral hemisphere|hemisphere]]) in important brain regions associated with the processing of emotion. These regions include:
*  [[middle temporal gyrus|Mid]] and  [[superior temporal gyrus|superior]]  [[transverse temporal gyrus|temporal gyri]]<ref>{{cite journal|last=Mitchell|first=R. L. C.|author2=Elliott, R.|author3= Barry, M.|author4= Cruttenden, A.|author5= Woodruff, P. W. R.|title=The neural response to emotional prosody, as revealed by functional magnetic resonance imaging|journal=Neuropsychologia|year=2003|volume=41|issue=10|pages=1410–1421|doi=10.1016/S0028-3932(03)00017-4|pmid=12757912|s2cid=18338901}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Leitman|first=D. I.|author2=Wolf, D. H.|author3= Ragland, J. D. |author4=Laukka, P. |author5=Loughead, J. |author6=Valdez, J. N. |author7=Gur, G. C.|title="It's not what you say, but how you say it": A reciprocal temporo-frontal network for affective prosody|journal=Frontiers in Human Neuroscience|year=2010|volume=4|pages=4–19|doi=10.3389/fnhum.2010.00019|pmid=20204074|pmc=2831710|doi-access=free}}</ref>
*  [[Insular cortex|Insulae]]<ref name = emotions>{{cite journal|last=Klasen|first=M.|author2=Kenworthy, C. A.|author3= Mathiak, K. A.|author4= Kircher, T. T. J.|author5= Mathiak, K.|title=Supramodal Representation of Emotions|journal=Journal of Neuroscience|year=2011|volume=31|issue=38|pages=13635–13643|doi=10.1523/jneurosci.2833-11.2011 |pmid=21940454 |pmc=6623280}}</ref><ref name="Towards a fronto-temporal neural network for the decoding of angry vocal expressions">{{cite journal|last=Frühholz|first=S.|author2=Grandjean, D. |title= Towards a fronto-temporal neural network for the decoding of angry vocal expressions|journal=NeuroImage|year=2012|volume=62|issue=3|pages=1658–1666|doi= 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.06.015 |pmid=22721630|s2cid=6589393}}</ref>
* [[Inferior frontal gyrus]]<ref name="Towards a fronto-temporal neural network for the decoding of angry vocal expressions" /><ref>{{cite journal|last=Ethofer|first=T.|author2=Bretscher, J.|author3= Gschwind, M.|author4= Kreifelts, B.|author5= Wildgruber, D.|author6= Vuilleumier, P.|title=Emotional voice areas: Anatomic location, functional properties, and structural connections revealed by combined fMRI/DTI|journal=Cerebral Cortex|year=2011|volume=22|issue=1|pages=191–200|doi=10.1093/cercor/bhr113|pmid=21625012|doi-access=free}}</ref>
* [[Basal ganglia]]<ref>{{cite journal|last=Pell|first=M. D.|author2=Leonard, C. L.|title=Processing emotional tone from speech in Parkinson's disease: A role for the basal ganglia|journal=Cognitive, Affective, & Behavioral Neuroscience|year=2003|volume=3|issue=4|pages=275–288|doi=10.3758/cabn.3.4.275|pmid=15040548|doi-access=free}}</ref>
* [[Amygdalae]]<ref name = emotions/><ref>{{cite journal|last=Frühholz|first=S.|author2=Grandjean, D.|title=Amygdala subregions differentially respond and rapidly adapt to threatening voices|journal=Cortex|year=2013|volume=49|issue=5|pages=1394–1403|doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2012.08.003|pmid=22938844|s2cid=21376073}}</ref>
Additional to the processing of memory and emotion, the amygdalae have important roles as “relevance detectors" for the discernment of relevant social information.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Sander|first=D.|author2=Grafman, J.|author3= Zalla, T.|title=The Human Amygdala: An Evolved System for Relevance Detection|journal=Reviews in the Neurosciences|year=2003|volume=14|issue=4|pages=303–316|doi=10.1515/revneuro.2003.14.4.303|pmid=14640318|s2cid=24811267}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Schirmer|first=A.|author2=Escoffier, N.|author3= Zysset, S.|author4= Koester, D.|author5= Striano, T.|author6= Friederici, A. D.|title=When vocal processing gets emotional: On the role of social orientation in relevance detection by the human amygdala|journal=NeuroImage|year=2008|volume=40|issue=3|pages=1402–1410|doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2008.01.018|pmid=18299209|s2cid=207168546}}</ref>  Therefore, these brain regions that deal with social relevance and vocal emotion are probable candidates for a neural network concerning accent-based group membership that would drive the affective processing of accents.

=== Prototype representation ===

The prototype representation approach stems from theories developed in the [[linguistics]] and [[cognitive psychology]] domains. It proposes that there are “prototypes” (i.e., internal representations) stored in the brain, which incoming information from the senses is compared against to facilitate categorisation.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Rosch|first=E.|title=Natural categories|journal=Cognitive Psychology|year=1973|volume=4|issue=3|pages=328–350|doi=10.1016/0010-0285(73)90017-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Valentine|first=T.|title=A unified account of the effects of distinctiveness, inversion, and race in face recognition|journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A|year=1991|volume=43|issue=2|pages=161–204|doi=10.1080/14640749108400966|pmid=1866456|s2cid=9159259}}</ref> Therefore, the own-accent bias is due to the fact that own-accents are similar to the prototype of "accent" hence are processed and categorised more easily than those other-accents that are dissimilar. This idea is supported by research showing that the further away a voice is from the average, (which is assumed to be a good representation of the internal prototype of accent) the more distinctive and less attractive it is rated, and the more activity is produced in the [[temporal voice areas]] (areas of the brain that deal with voice perception and accents).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Belin|first=P.|author2=Zatorre, R. J.|title='What', 'where' and 'how' in auditory cortex [letter to the editor]|journal=Nature Neuroscience|year=2000|volume=3|issue=10|pages=965–966|doi=10.1038/79890|pmid=11017161|s2cid=26749876}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Bestelmeyer|first=P. E. G.|author2=Belin, P.|author3= Grosbras, M-H.|title=Right temporal TMS impairs voice detection|journal=Current Biology|year=2011|volume=21|issue=20|pages=R838–R839|doi=10.1016/j.cub.2011.08.046|pmid=22032183|doi-access=free|bibcode=2011CBio...21.R838B }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Bestelmeyer|first=P. E. G.|author2=Latinus, M.|author3= Bruckert, L.|author4= Rouger, J.|author5= Crabbe, F.|author6= Belin, P.|title=Implicitly perceived vocal attractiveness modulates prefrontal cortex activity|journal=Cerebral Cortex|year=2011|volume=22|issue=6|pages=1263–1270|doi=10.1093/cercor/bhr204|pmid=21828348|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Bruckert|first=L.|author2=Bestelmeyer, P. E. G.|author3= Latinus, M. |author4=Rouger, J. |author5=Charest, I. |author6=Rousselet, G. A. |author7=Belin, P.|title=Vocal attractiveness increases by averaging|journal=Current Biology|year=2010|volume=20|issue=2|pages=116–120|doi=10.1016/j.cub.2009.11.034|pmid=20129047|doi-access=free|bibcode=2010CBio...20..116B }}</ref>

== Research into accent perception ==

Recent research has investigated the effects of accent on [[earwitness memory]] (similar to [[eyewitness memory]] but based on what a person heard rather than saw). The study showed that ear-witnesses were more likely to mistake offenders with a different accent than an own-accent, and that their judgements were less confident in reporting other-accent offenders compared to those with their own-accent.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Stevenage|first=S. V.|author2=Clarke, G.|author3= McNeill, A.|title=The "other-accent" effect in voice recognition|journal=Journal of Cognitive Psychology|year=2012|volume=24|issue=6|pages=647–653|doi=10.1080/20445911.2012.675321|s2cid=144037180}}</ref> The authors of the study present similarities between the own-accent bias and the  [[Cross-race effect|own-race bias]], which states that faces are more easily recognised by people of the same race (own-race) because those people have more experience (higher expertise) with them compared to faces of different races (other-race).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Chiroro|first=P.|author2=Valentine, T.|title=An investigation of the contact hypothesis of the own-race bias in face recognition|journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A|year=1995|volume=48|issue=4|pages=879–894|doi=10.1080/14640749508401421|s2cid=144656866}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Michel|first=C.|author2=Caldara, R.|author3= Rossion, B.|title=Same-race faces are perceived more holistically than other-race faces|journal=Visual Cognition|year=2006|volume=14|issue=1|pages=55–73|doi=10.1080/13506280500158761|s2cid=144198605}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Tanaka|first=J. W.|author2=Kiefer, M.|author3= Bukach, C. M.|title=A holistic account of the own-race effect in face recognition: evidence from a cross-cultural study|journal=Cognition|year=2004|volume=93|issue=1|pages=B1–B9|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2003.09.011|pmid=15110726|s2cid=15696105}}</ref> This is similar to the prototype representation theory of the own-accent bias (see above). Another study investigated the effects of teacher-accent on student learning. This research found that students recalled more information from lectures with teachers who had their own-accent and rated the own-accent teachers more favourably compared to those with an other-accent.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Gill|first=M. M.|title=Accent and stereotypes: Their effect on perceptions of teachers and lecture comprehension|journal=Journal of Applied Communication Research|year=1994|volume=22|issue=4|pages=348–361|doi=10.1080/00909889409365409}}</ref> Additionally, research focussing on the development of the own-accent bias in infants and children has shown that children are not only consistently able to differentiate between foreign- and native-accents<ref>{{cite journal|last=Floccia|first=C.|author2=Butler, J.|author3= Girard, F.|author4= Goslin, J.|title=Categorization of regional and foreign accent in 5- to 7-year-old British children|journal=International Journal of Behavioral Development|year=2009|volume=33|issue=4|pages=366–375|doi=10.1177/0165025409103871|hdl=10026.1/9958|s2cid=39722665|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Girard|first=F.|author2=Floccia, C.|author3= Goslin, J.|title=Perception and awareness of accents in young children|journal=British Journal of Developmental Psychology|year=2008|volume=26|issue=3|pages=409–433|doi=10.1348/026151007X251712|hdl=10026.1/9961|hdl-access=free}}</ref> but that infants and children prefer individuals who have a native accent compared to a foreign one, leading them to change their behaviour based on a speakers accent (e.g., accepting a toy off a native-accented speaker rather than a foreign-accented speaker).<ref name="Accent Trumps Race in Guiding Children's Social Preferences" /><ref>{{cite journal|last=Kinzler|first=K. D.|author2=Dupoux, E.|author3= Spelke, E. S.|title=The native language of social cognition|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|year=2007|volume=104|issue=30|pages=12577–12580|doi=10.1073/pnas.0705345104|pmid=17640881|pmc=1941511|bibcode=2007PNAS..10412577K|doi-access=free}}</ref>
